items.q: Which energy system fuels a marathon?
items.why: Students associate hard exercise with anaerobic systems. Long-duration steady-state work uses AEROBIC metabolism (oxygen + carbs/fats) - efficient but slower to ramp up. Anaerobic systems dominate brief intense efforts.

items.q: How long does the ATP-PC system last at maximum intensity?
items.why: Students overestimate the ATP-PC system. It provides instant energy but stores deplete in ~10 seconds (one sprint, one heavy lift). After that, glycolysis or aerobic systems take over.

items.q: What type of joint is the knee?
items.why: Students confuse knee with hip. Both are in the leg, but the hip is ball-and-socket (multi-directional); the knee is a hinge (mostly flexion-extension). Ball-and-socket joints have more range.

items.q: EPOC's slow component is mainly used to:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Clear lactate and restore glycogen. “Restore PCr” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: Fitness rebounding above baseline after recovery
items.why: Correct answer: Fitness rebounding above baseline after recovery. “Overtraining state” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: If post-exercise VO₂ averages 1.2 L min⁻¹ above rest for 30 min, EPOC volume (L) is?
items.why: The real figure is 36. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
